short.headline: 41.6% of Indian workers still in agriculture
short.dek: The share has fallen slowly from 63.1% in 1991, and even rose after 2019. Farming generates only 16.3% of GDP.
short.body: In 2025, roughly 41.6% of India’s workforce, more than two in every five workers, is still employed in agriculture, according to ILO modelled estimates. That is down from 63.1% in 1991, a decline of just over 20 percentage points in three decades. The shift out of farming has been frustratingly slow. Industry and services have not created enough jobs to absorb those leaving the land. Recent PLFS data even show a small reversal: agriculture’s worker share crept up from 42.4% in 2017–18 to 43.5% in 2023–24. Meanwhile, farming generates only 16.3% of national output, meaning each farm worker produces far less value than someone in industry or services. The gap, many hands, little output, is the central challenge.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: Picture this: in a room of 100 working Indians, 42 of them are farmers, farm labourers, fishers, or forest workers. That’s barely changed from 30 years ago when the number was 63. It means that despite all the cities, malls, and IT parks, the backbone of Indian work is still the plough. The problem is, all that toil creates only a tiny slice of the national pie, just about 16 rupees out of every 100. So a huge chunk of people are sharing a very small cake. Moving them into more productive work, making things, fixing things, providing services, is the single biggest thing India needs to get right. And right now, that move is stuck.
macha.soWhat: Until more Indians can find work outside the farm, their incomes will stay stuck, no matter how fast the economy grows.

article.title: Still on the farm: India's agricultural workforce
article.standfirst: About 41.6% of Indian workers remain in agriculture, down from 63.1% in 1991 but with a recent uptick, while the sector contributes only 16.3% of GDP.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## How many Indians still work in agriculture?

In 2025, 41.6% of India’s employed people work mainly in farming, forestry, or fishing. That means more than two in every five workers are still on the farm. The long-term trend is a slow decline: back in 1991, the figure stood at 63.1%. Over 34 years, the share has fallen by about 21.5 percentage points. But notice the pace, it took over three decades just to drop by a fifth. At this rate, even by 2040, a very large chunk of Indians will still be working the soil. The data here comes from the World Bank’s ILO modelled estimates, which smooth out year-to-year bumps and make long comparisons possible. One thing to remember: a falling share does not mean the absolute number of farmers is shrinking. Because India’s total workforce keeps growing, the number of people in agriculture has actually risen even as the percentage dropped.

## Why has the move out of farming been so slow?

The classic development path is for workers to move from farm to factory to office. In India, that shift has been slow and uneven. In 1991, agriculture held 63.1% of workers, industry just 14.6%, and services 22.3%. By 2025, agriculture is down to 41.6%, but industry barely grew to 25.8%, and services reached only 32.6%. The chart with three lines, agriculture falling gradually, industry rising modestly, services rising a bit more, shows that neither factories nor offices have absorbed the millions who should have left the land. The result: farming remains the largest employer, not because it’s attractive, but because other sectors have not grown enough to pull workers in. This is the textbook problem of jobless growth: the economy produces more, but without creating enough new non-farm jobs.

## Did the pandemic reverse the shift away from farming?

The most recent official survey data from the Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS) suggests that after 2019, agriculture’s share of workers actually rose. In 2017–18, it was 42.4%; by 2023–24, it reached 43.5%. Meanwhile, services fell from 32.6% to 31.6%, and industry stayed flat at 24.9%. The pandemic triggered a reverse migration, millions of casual workers in cities lost their jobs and returned to their villages, taking up whatever work was available, often farming or farm labour. Even after the immediate shock faded, many did not go back. The PLFS data captures this distressing reversal. It does not mean India has permanently abandoned the path of structural transformation, but it shows just how fragile the off-farm job market remains.

## Why do so many workers produce so little value?

Agriculture’s share of workers (41.6%) is nearly three times its share of the economy’s output. In 2024, farming contributed only 16.3% of gross value added, that is, the value of all goods and services produced, before taxes and subsidies. When you overlay the two series, the gap stares at you: a huge workforce producing a small slice of national income. This is the textbook definition of low labour productivity. Think of it this way: if every worker in agriculture produced the same as the average worker in the rest of the economy, either far fewer people would be needed in fields, or farming’s output share would be much larger. The reality is that too many Indians are sharing too little productive land, machinery, and know-how. Closing this gap is the central economic challenge.

## Which sectors actually drive the economy?

If you look at where national output comes from, the picture is almost the opposite of employment. In 2024, services generated 49.9% of GDP, industry 24.6%, and agriculture just 16.3%. In 1960, agriculture was the giant at 41.7%, and services were smaller at 38.8%. Over six decades, services have grown to dominate, while agriculture’s importance has shrivelled, yet its grip on jobs remains. Industry’s share has barely moved, from 20.8% to 24.6%. This mismatch, services as the biggest value creator but the smallest employer relative to its share, and agriculture still the biggest employer, means that growth has not been inclusive. The challenge is to grow in ways that pull workers into higher-productivity sectors.

## How does India compare with other countries?

In 1991, India and China had similar shares of workers in farming, India at 63.1%, China at 59.6%. Vietnam was even higher at 74%. Thirty-four years later, China’s share has plummeted to 21.7%, Vietnam’s to 25%, and even Bangladesh has fallen more sharply to 44.3%, though it started higher. Indonesia is at 27.3%. The world average is 25.8%. India, at 41.6%, stands out for how slowly the transition has happened. This is not because other countries started richer; Vietnam in 1991 was poorer than India but created manufacturing jobs that pulled millions out of fields. India’s manufacturing sector has not boomed in the same way, leaving workers stuck in low-productivity farm labour. The cross-country chart puts India’s sluggishness in stark relief.

## What does labour productivity look like overall?

A broader measure of labour productivity, GDP per person employed, expressed in constant 2021 international dollars, gives a sense of the overall outcome. In 1991, each Indian worker produced about $6,601 (PPP). By 2024, that number had climbed to $24,430. That is a more than threefold rise, which is progress. But while the average is up, the average hides vast differences between sectors. The gap between a farm worker’s output and a services worker’s output is immense. This chart does not show the gap directly, but it reminds us that raising overall productivity means shifting workers out of sectors where they produce very little and into sectors where they produce more. The sluggish farm exit is why India’s aggregate productivity remains modest.

## How long has agriculture dominated Indian work?

Historical reconstructions put the share of Indian workers in agriculture at 71.9% as recently as 1960. By 2010, it had inched down to 54.7%. The very long view, pieced together from academic datasets, shows a stubborn persistence. Even the green revolution and industrialisation after independence did not quickly empty the fields. The decline accelerated after 1991, but the sheer starting point of over 70% means that absolute numbers of farm workers kept growing for decades. India started with an extreme reliance on farming, and reversing that requires sustained, broad-based job creation outside agriculture.

## Is the jobs guarantee scheme a sign of farm distress?

When farming or casual non-farm work dries up, rural households turn to MGNREGA, which guarantees up to 100 days of manual wage work per year. The person-days created under the scheme are a barometer of rural distress. The data, running monthly from 2013, shows peaks during drought years and economic shocks. In April 2013, 186.8 million person-days were created. By April 2026, that number was 109.9 million. The recent trend is down from the peak, but the demand remains high, especially in poor agricultural states. MGNREGA is not a measure of farm employment directly, but when agriculture’s worker share rises, as it did after 2019, MGNREGA demand often stays elevated, signalling that many rural workers are stitching together a livelihood from several sources, with the jobs guarantee as a last resort.

## What do rural workers actually earn?

Daily wages in rural areas, where most agricultural workers live, reveal the floor under livelihoods. For men, the average rural daily wage in June 2025 was ₹454. Women earned ₹322, a gap of about ₹132 per day. Over the long run, men’s wages have risen from just ₹72 in 1998, and women’s from ₹163 in 2013. That sounds like a big increase, but these are nominal rupees, not adjusted for inflation. In real terms, the improvement has been much smaller. Also, the gender gap is stark: women consistently earn less for the same type of casual labour. These wages are what farm workers and other rural workers actually take home. When the farm cannot support a family, these daily rates determine whether they can survive without migrating.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: This page explores how much of India's workforce remains in agriculture, why the shift out of farming has been slow, and the wide gap between farm employment and the sector's modest contribution to national output.

selectedDataPoints.label: Employment in agriculture, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: The headline number that directly answers the page question.

selectedDataPoints.label: Employment in agriculture, earliest
selectedDataPoints.reason: Shows the starting point for the long-term decline.

selectedDataPoints.label: Agriculture, value added, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: Reveals the productivity gap when compared with employment share.

selectedDataPoints.reason: Captures the recent uptick in PLFS data.

selectedDataPoints.reason: Benchmarks India's slow transition against a fast-mover.

pullQuotes.quote: In 2025, more than two in five working Indians are still on the farm, 41.6%, down from 63.1% in 1991.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Employment in agriculture, latest and earliest

pullQuotes.quote: Farming creates only 16.3% of the country's output, yet absorbs 41.6% of its workers.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Agriculture, value added, latest and Employment in agriculture, latest

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of 100 working Indians. The employment share in agriculture tells you how many of them are mainly farming, fishing, or forestry. It is the percentage of all workers whose main job is in that sector. For example, if the share is 41.6%, it means about 42 out of every 100 workers are farmers or farm labourers. This number counts people, not hours worked, and it uses each person’s primary occupation, so a farmer who also does a little trading is still counted as an agricultural worker.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It directly answers the page question and shows how dependent India remains on farming for livelihoods.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Imagine two workers: one in a factory making mobile phones, another in a field tending wheat. The factory worker produces more value in a day than the farm worker, because machines and technology amplify their effort. Labour productivity measures this: it is the average economic value (GDP) produced per worker. When many workers are in farming with limited land and simple tools, overall productivity is low. Moving workers to higher-productivity sectors raises overall income.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It explains why having so many people in agriculture is a problem: they produce a small share of national income.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of the economy as a cake. The GDP share of a sector tells you how much of the cake that sector bakes. If agriculture’s value added is 16.3% of GDP, it means about 16 rupees out of every 100 rupees of national output come from farming, including crops, livestock, and related raw materials. Value added is the sector’s contribution after subtracting the cost of inputs like seeds and fertiliser. It is not the same as the sector’s revenue; it’s the net addition to the economy.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It contrasts sharply with the employment share, revealing the productivity gap.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act is like a government promise: if you live in a village and ask for work, you are guaranteed at least 100 days of manual labour each year, paid at daily rates. The work is physical, digging ponds, building roads, planting trees. Person-days created count the total number of workdays provided. One person-day means one person worked for one day. High numbers often mean not enough regular jobs, so people turn to this safety net.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: When farm jobs and other rural work dry up, people turn to MGNREGA; high demand signals distress.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Picture a village where almost everyone farms. Slowly, a few start making bricks, then a factory opens, then a shop, then an IT kiosk. Over decades, fewer people are in the fields and more in manufacturing and services. That’s structural transformation, the long-term shift of workers from low-productivity agriculture to higher-productivity sectors. As this happens, a country’s overall output per person rises, and incomes grow. The process is not automatic; it needs roads, education, and new industries that demand labour.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India’s transformation has been unusually slow, keeping a large share of workers in low-productivity agriculture.

visualizationDecisions.reason: A long annual line shows the slow decline clearly; annual data avoids seasonal noise.
Three lines on one chart with annual points reveal the relative shifts without clutter.
Short window highlights the recent blip; PLFS data is annual for this period.
Overlaying two lines efficiently shows the divergence between employment and GDP shares.
Three lines on a percentage chart show composition changes smoothly.
Multiple country lines; limiting to six series keeps the chart readable on mobile.
A steady rise is best shown with a continuous line.
The long-run decline is clear with a single line from 1960 to 2010.
Monthly data captures seasonal spikes and shocks; a line reveals trends and volatility.
Monthly wages show seasonal patterns; two lines on one chart highlight the gender gap.

chartExplainers.title: Share of Indians working in agriculture
chartExplainers.takeaway: The share of workers in agriculture has fallen from 63.1% in 1991 to 41.6% in 2025, a slow decline of just 21.5 percentage points over three decades.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart plots the percentage of India’s total employed population whose primary job is in agriculture, forestry, or fishing, using ILO modelled estimates from the World Bank. The line starts at 63.1% in 1991 and descends gradually, with small plateaus, to reach 41.6% in 2025. The downward trend reflects millions of workers slowly moving into industry and services, but the pace is sluggish. Even with this decline, agriculture remains the single largest employer. The chart also hints at periods of stagnation, for instance, the share barely moved in the early 2000s. It is important to remember that this is a percentage: as India’s workforce has grown from around 300 million to over 500 million, the absolute number of agricultural workers has actually increased, even as their share fell.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This is the headline chart that directly answers the page question with the most recent data.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line shows the percentage of all employed Indians working in agriculture each year. Find the latest point (2025) for the current number.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse a falling share with falling absolute numbers; the total number of farmers may still be rising.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a single line with large labelled data points at start, end, and key inflections; minimal gridlines.

chartExplainers.title: The slow shift from farm to factory to office
chartExplainers.takeaway: Industry and services have not grown fast enough: agriculture still employs 41.6% of workers, while industry and services together account for 58.4%.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart stacks three lines, one per broad sector: agriculture (falling), industry (rising slowly), and services (rising faster but still modestly). Starting in 1991, the three lines show the classic scissors shape, but the crossing point, when another sector overtakes agriculture, never arrives. Agriculture remains the thickest line at the end, at 41.6%. Industry grew from 14.6% to 25.8%, and services from 22.3% to 32.6%. The pace of shift determines whether the average worker’s productivity can rise. India’s lines move sluggishly, especially industry, which has never boomed as it did in East Asia. As a result, millions of workers are stuck in low-productivity farming. The chart is a visual summary of India’s incomplete structural transformation.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It reveals why the agricultural workforce share remains high: the non-farm sectors are not absorbing enough labour.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Trace each line from left to right. Note that the agriculture line (green) stays on top. Compare the steepness of declines in other countries for context.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume the three shares add to 100%; minor discrepancies exist due to a small ‘not classified’ category.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use three clearly distinguishable coloured lines with simple labels; the axes should be large enough to read on a small screen.

chartExplainers.title: The post-2019 reversal
chartExplainers.takeaway: PLFS data shows agriculture’s worker share rose from 42.4% in 2017–18 to 43.5% in 2023–24, reversing the long-term decline.
chartExplainers.detail: Based on the Periodic Labour Force Survey, this chart zooms into the most recent years. The agriculture line, which had been drifting downward, ticks up after 2019, ending at 43.5%. Services, which had been rising, drops from 32.6% to 31.6%. Industry remains flat at 24.9%. The pandemic-induced lockdowns crushed urban casual jobs, pushing millions of migrant workers back to their villages. Many took up farm work out of desperation, and the recovery has not fully re-absorbed them. This blip is a stark reminder that India’s structural transformation is fragile; a single large shock can undo years of gradual progress. It also raises a question: is this a temporary wobble, or does it signal a deeper weakness in India’s non-farm job creation?
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It highlights a worrying sign, the decades-long march out of agriculture may be stalling.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the tiny uptick in the agriculture line after 2019 and the corresponding dip in services. The scale is magnified compared to the long-term chart.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not treat PLFS data as perfectly comparable with the ILO modelled series; survey definitions differ slightly.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep the time axis uncluttered with annual markers; a small callout box can highlight the reversal.

chartExplainers.title: Half the workers, a fraction of the output
chartExplainers.takeaway: Agriculture employs 41.6% of workers but contributes only 16.3% of GDP, a productivity gap that widens over time.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines on one chart tell a stark story. The employment share (orange) falls slowly from over 60% to 41.6%, while the GDP share (blue) plunges from 41.7% in 1960 to just 16.3% in 2024. The space between the lines, the gap, is a measure of how little value each farmer produces relative to the average. In the 1960s, the two lines were close; today, they are far apart. This divergence happened because while some workers left farming, the sector’s output grew much slower than the rest of the economy, so its GDP share shrank. Meanwhile, the number of workers in agriculture remained high. The result: many people share a shrinking pie. This chart captures the heart of India’s employment problem.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It directly visualises the core mismatch: agriculture is overstaffed and underproductive.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Watch the growing distance between the two lines. The employment line stays stubbornly high; the GDP line drops.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not interpret the percentage point gap as a direct productivity ratio; it compares shares, not levels.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a dual-axis or clearly labelled single-axis chart; annotate the gap at the most recent year.

chartExplainers.title: Where the economy's value comes from
chartExplainers.takeaway: Services generate half the GDP (49.9%), while agriculture’s contribution has halved to 16.3% since 1960.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the composition of India’s gross value added, essentially, which sectors produce the national output. In 1960, agriculture was the largest at 41.7%, services 38.8%, industry 20.8%. Over six decades, the lines cross and recross. Agriculture slides down to 16.3%, while services rise to dominate at 49.9%. Industry’s share inches up to 24.6%. The crossover between agriculture and services happened around the 1980s, and since then services have pulled away. The chart makes clear that while farming has lost its economic weight, it has not shed workers at the same pace. This asymmetry, services led growth, but not employment, is a defining feature of India’s development path.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides the output side of the story, completing the picture started by the employment charts.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Follow the lines from 1960 to 2024. Note the crossing point and the expanding gap between services and agriculture.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not equate the GDP share with the number of workers; the two move independently.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use three distinct colours; label the most recent year’s values clearly to avoid clutter.

chartExplainers.title: How India compares on leaving the farm
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s agricultural employment share is nearly twice the world average of 25.8% and far above China’s 21.7% or Vietnam’s 25%.
chartExplainers.detail: This multi-line chart places India alongside five comparators: Bangladesh, China, Indonesia, Vietnam, and the world average. All started with high agricultural shares in 1991, but their paths diverged sharply. China dived from 59.6% to 21.7%, Vietnam from 74% to 25%, both driven by manufacturing booms. India’s line drops more slowly and remains highest among the group except Bangladesh (44.3%). The world average line sits at 25.8%, showing how much India is an outlier among large economies. The chart is a powerful argument: India’s slow structural transformation is not inevitable, similar countries managed it much faster. It points to a missing ingredient: a robust, labour-intensive manufacturing sector.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It benchmarks India’s performance and shows that rapid exit from farming is possible.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the slopes. Steeper lines mean faster transformation. India’s line is flatter than most.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not ignore starting points; China and Vietnam began with similar shares, but their economic strategies differed.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Limit to six lines; use dashed lines for neighbours and a solid line for India, with a clear legend.

chartExplainers.title: Output per worker
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s overall labour productivity has risen from $6,601 in 1991 to $24,430 in 2024 (constant PPP), but is still low relative to potential.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart plots real GDP per person employed, a standard measure of how much value each worker produces on average. The line rises steadily from 1991 to 2024, more than tripling. But the absolute level remains modest. In a sense, the line tracks the progress of the entire workforce, including those in high-productivity services and low-productivity agriculture. Because agriculture is a large chunk, it drags down the average. The implication is clear: if more workers moved to higher-productivity sectors, the line would rise faster. The chart does not show sectoral breakdown, but combined with the previous charts, it drives home the point that India's overall productivity gains are held back by the large farm workforce.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It links the employment structure to overall economic performance and living standards.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line’s upward slope indicates improving worker output. The latest value gives a sense of the economy's productive capacity per worker.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is total economy productivity, not agriculture-specific. The within-sector gap is much larger.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple line chart with a single color and a few labelled points; keep the currency scale abbreviation clear.

chartExplainers.title: The very long view
chartExplainers.takeaway: From 1960 to 2010, India’s agricultural employment share fell from 71.9% to 54.7%, a decline of just 17 percentage points over 50 years.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart draws on long-run historical reconstructions to show that as recently as 1960, nearly three-quarters of Indian workers were in agriculture. The line descends slowly through the decades, picking up speed only after the 1990s. By 2010, it had reached 54.7%. The dataset ends there, so the most recent decade is missing, but the long-run decline is clear. importantly, even in 2010, more than half of the workforce was still farming, a level many other countries had left behind generations earlier. This chart provides the deep context: India’s dependence on agriculture is not a recent phenomenon, but a centuries-old structure that is proving exceptionally hard to reshape.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It anchors the current numbers in a century of slow change, highlighting how deeply agriculture is embedded in India’s labour market.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line descends from upper left to lower right. Note the gently sloping descent, which steepens after 1990.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The series ends in 2010, so it does not capture the recent decade’s developments. Use only for long-term perspective.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple line chart with a sparse time axis; label the start and end points.

chartExplainers.title: Rural work demand: the NREGA signal
chartExplainers.takeaway: MGNREGA person-days created have fallen from a peak of 186.8 million in April 2013 to 109.9 million in April 2026, but remain high, signalling persistent rural distress.
chartExplainers.detail: This monthly line chart shows the total person-days of work provided under MGNREGA, a demand-driven employment guarantee. The series exhibits sharp spikes and troughs, often linked to monsoon failures or economic shocks. The peak in 2013 followed a drought; the dip in recent years may reflect some improvement, but the level remains substantial. When farm and non-farm employment options shrink, rural households sign up for this manual work. The chart therefore serves as a barometer of how many people are unable to find adequate other work. The recent trend, when read alongside the rising agricultural employment share, suggests that for many, farming and NREGA work are complementary survival strategies.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides a real-time indicator of rural labour market stress, complementing the structural employment data.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Spikes indicate periods of high distress; the overall level, rather than month-to-month changes, indicates the underlying need.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Person-days are not jobs; they count total workdays provided. Also, demand can be constrained by budget and administrative capacity.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a line chart with monthly points but simplify to annual or quarterly averages for a cleaner visual; highlight the peak.

chartExplainers.title: Rural wages
chartExplainers.takeaway: Average daily rural wages in June 2025 were ₹454 for men and ₹322 for women, revealing a large gender gap and modest nominal levels.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines track the average daily wage for casual rural labourers: men’s wages (starting 1998) and women’s (starting 2013). Men’s wages have risen from ₹72 to ₹454, while women’s have moved from ₹163 to ₹322. The gender gap persists, with women earning about 70% of what men do. These wages are important because they represent the cash income of millions of agricultural labourers. The rise over time looks impressive, but inflation erodes much of the gain. In real terms, the improvement is less striking. The chart also hints at seasonal patterns; wages often dip during the lean agricultural season. Together with the NREGA data, it sketches the material reality of rural work: insecure, poorly paid, and highly gendered.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It reveals the actual earnings that underpin rural livelihoods, directly linked to the agricultural workforce.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines: one for men (higher) and one for women (lower). Check the latest values on the right. Note the persistent vertical distance.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These are nominal wages, not adjusted for inflation. Real wage growth has been slower.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use two contrasting colours; label the latest monthly value for each gender. Keep the time axis readable by showing a rolling average if needed.

sectionVisualMap.heading: How many Indians still work in agriculture?
Why has the move out of farming been so slow?
Did the pandemic reverse the shift away from farming?
Why do so many workers produce so little value?
Which sectors actually drive the economy?
How does India compare with other countries?
What does labour productivity look like overall?
How long has agriculture dominated Indian work?
Is the jobs guarantee scheme a sign of farm distress?
What do rural workers actually earn?

sourceNotes: Employment data: ILO modelled estimates, accessed via World Bank (SL.AGR.EMPL.ZS, SL.IND.EMPL.ZS, SL.SRV.EMPL.ZS).
Value added: World Bank (NV.AGR.TOTL.ZS, NV.IND.TOTL.ZS, NV.SRV.TOTL.ZS).
PLFS data: Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI), India.
Long-run agricultural employment share: Herrendorf et al. and GGDC-10 data, via Our World in Data.
Rural wages: India Data Hub blended series (LAWRAGGAVG13M, LAWRWTTAVG13M).
caveats: ILO modelled estimates are based on labour force surveys and may differ from official Indian PLFS figures; they smooth year-on-year fluctuations.
PLFS data cover a shorter period (from 2017–18) and use slightly different sector definitions, so direct comparisons with ILO series should be cautious.
The long-run chart (1960–2010) comes from historical reconstructions with possible methodological breaks and does not include the most recent decade.
Rural wages are nominal; real wage growth is slower after adjusting for inflation.
MGNREGA person-days reflect both demand and government budget/administrative capacity, so low numbers do not necessarily mean low distress.
Cross-country comparisons use the same ILO methodology, but labour force definitions and informal sector coverage may vary.

GDP per person employed (labour productivity), earliest
Share of employment in agriculture (long run): Share of employment in agriculture (Herrendorf et al. and GGDC-10 data) - 1960
Share of employment in agriculture (long run): Share of employment in agriculture (Herrendorf et al. and GGDC-10 data) - 2010

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: About 41.6% of Indian workers remain in agriculture, a share that fell slowly from 63.1% in 1991 and even rose slightly in recent years, while the sector produces only 16.3% of GDP, revealing a large productivity gap.
requiredConcepts: Define employment share in agriculture: the percentage of all employed people whose main job is in farming, forestry, or fishing.
Explain the unit: percent of total employment, not absolute numbers, and the denominator is all employed persons.
Explain the main chart: a line chart of India's agricultural employment share since 1991, showing the long-term decline from 63.1% to 41.6%.
Explain the most important supporting chart: the productivity gap chart overlaying employment share and GDP share, highlighting that agriculture employs far more workers than its economic output.

State the most important caveat: the data comes from surveys (ILO modelled estimates or PLFS) and may miss informal, seasonal, or multiple-job workers; definitions of 'agriculture' may differ slightly between sources.

sectionPlan.heading: How many Indians still work in agriculture?
sectionPlan.job: Give the headline number and the long-term trend from 1991 to 2025
conceptsToExplain: ILO modelled estimate vs survey data
sectionPlan.caveat: These are ILO modelled estimates based on labour force surveys; they may differ from official Indian PLFS figures.

sectionPlan.heading: Why has the move out of farming been so slow?
sectionPlan.job: Show that industry and services have not absorbed workers fast enough, and explain the classic structural transformation
conceptsToExplain: Structural transformation: the shift from farm to factory to office
Absorption of labour in non-farm sectors
sectionPlan.caveat: The chart uses ILO modelled data which smooths out year-on-year fluctuations; actual yearly data may show more volatility.

sectionPlan.heading: Did the pandemic reverse the shift away from farming?
sectionPlan.job: Highlight the post-2019 rise in agriculture employment share using PLFS data
sectionPlan.caveat: PLFS data cover a shorter period and may not be directly comparable with ILO estimates; they show a sharper recent reversal.

sectionPlan.heading: Why do so many workers produce so little value?
sectionPlan.job: Explain the productivity gap by comparing employment share and GDP share
sectionPlan.caveat: The GDP share dates from 2024, employment share from 2025; they are from different series but the gap is persistent.

sectionPlan.heading: Which sectors actually drive the economy?
sectionPlan.job: Show GDP shares of agriculture, industry, and services over time
sectionPlan.caveat: GDP shares add up to roughly 100%, but minor discrepancies exist due to taxes and subsidies.

sectionPlan.heading: How does India compare with other countries?
sectionPlan.job: Benchmark India against China, Vietnam, Bangladesh, Indonesia, and the world
sectionPlan.caveat: Comparisons use ILO modelled estimates; actual survey definitions and informal employment may differ across countries.

sectionPlan.heading: What does labour productivity look like overall?
sectionPlan.job: Show GDP per person employed (total economy) and link to why moving out of agriculture matters
lockedNumbersToUse: GDP per person employed (labour productivity), latest
GDP per person employed (labour productivity), earliest
conceptsToExplain: Labour productivity in constant PPP terms
Aggregate productivity as a proxy for sectoral gaps
sectionPlan.caveat: This is total economy productivity, not agriculture-specific; it illustrates the general level but not the within-sector gap.

sectionPlan.heading: How long has agriculture dominated Indian work?
sectionPlan.job: Provide historical context with a very long-run chart from OWID
lockedNumbersToUse: Share of employment in agriculture (long run): Share of employment in agriculture (Herrendorf et al. and GGDC-10 data)
sectionPlan.caveat: Long-run data comes from historical reconstructions and may have breaks in methodology; the series ends in 2010.

sectionPlan.heading: Is the jobs guarantee scheme a sign of farm distress?
sectionPlan.job: Show NREGA person-days created as a proxy for rural work demand
lockedNumbersToUse: Person-days of work created under MGNREGA, latest
Person-days of work created under MGNREGA, earliest
conceptsToExplain: MGNREGA as a demand-driven safety net
Person-days as a measure of work provided
sectionPlan.caveat: NREGA demand reflects not only farm distress but also implementation and funding; person-days data is monthly and seasonal.

sectionPlan.heading: What do rural workers actually earn?
sectionPlan.job: Show rural wage rates for men and women, highlighting the low level and gender gap
lockedNumbersToUse: Daily average rural wage rate, men (blended series), latest
Daily average rural wage rate, men (blended series), earliest
Daily average rural wage rate, women (blended series), latest
Daily average rural wage rate, women (blended series), earliest
sectionPlan.caveat: These are nominal wages; inflation erodes real purchasing power over time.

chartPlan.title: Share of Indians working in agriculture
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It directly answers the headline question with a single clear trend.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line shows the percentage of all employed Indians working in agriculture each year. The latest point (2025) reads 41.6%. The earliest (1991) was 63.1%. The gap between them is the decline over three decades.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse this with the absolute number of farmers. The share can fall even if the absolute number stays the same or rises, if total employment grows faster.

chartPlan.title: The slow shift from farm to factory to office
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It shows the three-sector structural change and why agriculture remains large.
chartPlan.howToRead: Three lines: agriculture (falling slowly), industry (rising gradually), services (rising more). At the end, agriculture is still the largest at 41.6%, services at 32.6%, industry at 25.8%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume industry or services are absorbing all the workers leaving agriculture; many still stay in farming due to lack of alternatives.

chartPlan.title: The post-2019 reversal
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It highlights a worrying recent trend: agriculture's share rose from 42.4% (2017-18) to 43.5% (2023-24) in PLFS data.
chartPlan.howToRead: The chart uses PLFS survey data from 2017-18 onwards. Note how the agriculture line dips and then rises after 2019, while services falls slightly from 32.6% to 31.6%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume this is a permanent reversal; it may reflect pandemic disruptions and subsequent recovery patterns.

chartPlan.title: Half the workers, a fraction of the output
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It starkly visualises the productivity gap: many workers produce little value.
chartPlan.howToRead: One line shows agriculture's share of workers (41.6% in 2025), the other shows its share of GDP (16.3% in 2024). The wide gap signals low output per worker.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not interpret the gap in percentage points as the productivity difference in absolute terms; it is a share comparison.

chartPlan.title: Where the economy's value comes from
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It contextualises agriculture's small GDP share relative to services and industry.
chartPlan.howToRead: Three lines showing sectoral value added as % of GDP. Services dominates at 49.9% (2024), industry 24.6%, agriculture 16.3%. Agriculture's line has fallen from 41.7% in 1960.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not equate value added with employment; services contribute half the GDP but employ only about a third.

chartPlan.title: How India compares on leaving the farm
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It benchmarks India's transformation speed against peers.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multiple lines: each country's agricultural employment share. In 2025, India is at 41.6%, Bangladesh 44.3%, Indonesia 27.3%, China 21.7%, Vietnam 25.0%, world average 25.8%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not ignore starting points. China and Vietnam had similar shares in 1991 but transformed much faster; India's decline was more gradual.

chartPlan.title: Output per worker
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It shows the overall productivity gain, implying the need for more non-farm jobs.
chartPlan.howToRead: GDP per person employed in constant 2021 PPP dollars. From $6,601 in 1991 to $24,430 in 2024. The rise is steady but still modest.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is total economy productivity, not agriculture-specific. The gap between sectors is even wider.

chartPlan.title: The very long view
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It provides historical depth: from 71.9% in 1960 to 54.7% in 2010.
chartPlan.howToRead: A single line going back to 1960, ending at 2010. The decline accelerated post-2000 but remains high.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The series ends in 2010; it does not capture the most recent decade. Use this for long-term perspective, not current numbers.

chartPlan.title: Rural work demand: the NREGA signal
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It reveals distress: when farm and non-farm jobs are scarce, rural households turn to the jobs guarantee.
chartPlan.howToRead: Monthly person-days created under MGNREGA. The latest value is 109.93 million (April 2026), down from a peak of 186.84 million in April 2013. Spikes often coincide with drought or economic shocks.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Person-days are not employment; they represent days of work provided. Also, demand is influenced by government budget and administrative capacity.

chartPlan.title: Rural wages
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It shows the floor under farm and rural livelihoods and the large gender gap.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: men's average daily rural wage (₹454 in June 2025) and women's (₹322). Men's wages have risen from ₹72 in 1998; women's data starts later at ₹163 in 2013.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These are nominal wages, not adjusted for inflation. The real purchasing power growth is smaller.

visualizationDecisions.reason: A long annual line shows the slow decline clearly; annual data avoids seasonal noise.
Three lines on one chart with annual points reveal the relative shifts without clutter.
PLFS data is annual; a shorter window highlights the recent blip.
Overlaying two lines with different axes (or dual-axis) efficiently shows the divergence.
Three lines on a percentage chart show composition changes smoothly.
Multiple country lines on one chart; avoid more than six series for readability.
A steady rise is best shown with a continuous line.
The long-run decay is clear with a single line from 1960 to 2010.
Monthly data captures seasonal spikes and shocks; a line reveals trends and volatility.
Monthly wages show seasonal patterns; two lines on one chart highlight the gender gap.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Think of 100 working Indians. The employment share in agriculture tells you how many of them are mainly farming, fishing, or forestry. It is the percentage of all workers whose main job is in that sector.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It directly answers the page question and shows how dependent India remains on farming for livelihoods.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: It is a measure of how much economic value (GDP) a country generates per worker on average. If productivity in a sector is low, each worker produces less value. This often happens in farming when many people share limited land and machinery.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It explains why having so many people in agriculture is a problem: they produce a small share of national income.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Think of the economy as a cake. The GDP share of a sector tells you how much of the cake that sector bakes. If agriculture’s share is 16.3%, it means about 16 rupees out of every 100 rupees of national output come from farming.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It contrasts sharply with the employment share, revealing the productivity gap.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act is a government scheme that promises at least 100 days of wage work each year to every rural household that asks for it. The work is manual and unskilled, like digging ponds or building roads. Person-days created measure the work actually provided.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: When farm jobs and other rural work dry up, people turn to MGNREGA; high demand signals distress.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: This is the long-term process of a country’s workforce shifting from farming (agriculture) to factories (industry) to offices and services. As an economy gets richer, fewer people work in fields and more work in manufacturing or services.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India’s transformation has been unusually slow, keeping a large share of workers in low-productivity agriculture.

proseRules: No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number

evidence.question: What share of Indians still work in agriculture?

theme.label: Society, work, education, and services
readerQuestions: What basic capability is being measured?
How many people does the percentage represent?
Is this access, usage, quality, or outcome?
Which caveat stops the chart from being overclaimed?

plannedCharts.title: Share of Indians working in agriculture
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline: how much of the workforce is still on the farm.

plannedCharts.title: The slow shift from farm to factory to office
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The classic development path, and how far India still has to go.

plannedCharts.title: The post-2019 reversal
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Recent PLFS data shows agriculture's share rising again, a warning sign.

plannedCharts.title: Half the workers, a fraction of the output
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The core problem: agriculture employs far more people than the value it produces.

plannedCharts.title: Where the economy's value comes from
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The mirror image: services dominate output while agriculture shrinks.

plannedCharts.title: How India compares on leaving the farm
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: China and Vietnam moved workers off farms much faster than India.

plannedCharts.title: Output per worker
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Why moving out of agriculture matters: productivity.

plannedCharts.title: The very long view
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Agriculture's grip on Indian work over more than a century.

plannedCharts.title: Rural work demand: the NREGA signal
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: When farm and non-farm work fall short, rural Indians turn to the jobs guarantee.

plannedCharts.title: Rural wages
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: What rural work actually pays, the floor under farm livelihoods.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
